title: [ci skip] System-test: Begin list of needed high-level tests

comments.body: @edsantiago @ypu PTAL what I have so far.  This can be added to more later, it is just a starting place.  I tried to write workflows that will self-validate (i.e. final test commands prove former commands were successful).

Hi @cevich 
Checked the items in the doc. Seems we can just copy some tests code from e2e directly except the last one (help). The test case under e2e is named with the subcommand. We need update the AfterEach() and BeforeEach() and adjust the steps(may remove some prepare steps as the test cases in e2e is separated) to fit the test cases.
For example, the second test case for stop and wait can be build with following test cases in e2e:
  - podman create container based on a remote image
  - podman start single container by name
  - podman exec simple command
  - podman logs for container
  - Podman wait(need some update for status and wait time)

Need add podman bud related functions like copy Dockerfile and update the Dockerfile and so on. But we already have these functions buildah testsuit. So it should not be hard.

I will take 7 days vacation in China for our spring festival from tomorrow. So I will not have time to init the pullreq until then. Maybe we can start with test case two as all needed common functions are ready. And I will start work on the bud related functions and other test cases as soon as I come back. Thanks for understanding.

Hi @cevich 
I feel I start to understand why you keep saying that golang and ginkgo is too high level. Seems you want to find a way that can test the binary directly in a system without any further request such like python dependence and bats support and so on. So the user can test podman like they using it in their env quickly.

But in my opinion this is why we should using ginkgo. 
First of all, system test is not the unit test. So it should be run under a certain system which means the rpm or package of podman should be build in a build system already. And the tests is not request more package than the podman binary except ginkgo which can be get easily with go get. So it has very low chance this will break the build process.

Second, if you look deep into BDD style testing, you should see it try to separate the test scenario and test code. So everyone can add test cases to it even for who can not coding. Ginkgo may not doing that in a very obvious way like other BDD style test framework which has separated feature file and code. But we have our e2e test for it. Each e2e test is a simple test step and if you grep the e2e test "It" items you can get all operates we already support, and people can make up system tests with simple copy and paste with it. Or users can just provide some description of the test case like what you did in this doc and we can translate it into a case quickly based what we already have in e2e tests.

So this is my reason for choice ginkgo for system test. As seems your pullreq for bash is still not ready. Maybe I can have a try for this? Looking forward for your feedback. :)

@ypu yes your understanding is correct.  If we are testing user running shell commands, then test should just run shell commands.  There is no better way to run shell commands than...using a shell :smile: 

I know about BDD.  The middle term, "Driven" is key:  You're suppose to write the tests BEFORE writing the code, so we are using it backwards without this benefit.  I also found the syntax-sugar of ginko/gomega to be very confusing and get in the way.  It does not follow standard Gherkin terms or feature descriptions.  Worse, the deeply nested-function style breaks up the scenario rules such that it's very hard for a newcomer to read/understand.

I agree with you debugging tests requires understanding them to some degree.  However, with so many tests and different people working on them, the extraneous ginkgo (and BDD in general) syntax seems to just get in the way.  It may be fine to use at the integration-test level, I'm not arguing that.

Rather, we want system-test to be as simple and boring as possible.  We should not spend lots of time/effort on them, nor should any future (new) developer.  We must focus most of our efforts writing/testing at the unit and integration level.  System-test is simply final gate before release.  It should always pass.  If system-tests ever fail, they should fail because there is serious/major problem we did not catch at unit/integration level.

Simply put:  System-test failure -> write new/better unit or integration test

Still, we are not 100% committed to BATS or similar framework yet, this PR is only about writing notes about requirements and planning :smile:
lolz...ci/prow/validate is suppose to fail...my commits are messed up here, fixing...
Good start @cevich As I'm sure you're aware, I think you just scratching the surface with the number and types of tests we should have.  Just a few suggested grammar things through out.
@TomSweeneyRedHat Thank you so much for taking a look, yes just a starting-place.  And thanks for the reminder about adding cards, I'll add doing that, to my higher-priority TODO list :smile:
@TomSweeneyRedHat okay, got all your suggestions and even opened a jira card:  RUN-303 :smile:   PTAL when you get a chance.  Thanks.
